title: Ansari-Renani (2016) — 'An investigation of organic sheep and goat production by nomad pastoralists in southern Iran' (Pastoralism: Research, Policy and Practice, journal article, open access)

files.name: ansari-renani-2016-organic-sheep-goat-nomad-pastoralists.pdf
files.notes: Author: Hamid R. Ansari-Renani. Pastoralism 6:8 (2016). DOI 10.1186/s13570-016-0056-y. CC BY 4.0. Field study of 30 nomad (Siahjel sub-tribe of the Raen tribe) households in Baft district, Kerman province.

time_coverage: 2012-2015 fieldwork (published 2016); cites FAO 2014 national livestock totals
notes: Directly answers project brief bucket 2 (nomadic/tribal pastoral economy, livestock-herd-size estimates). Key quantified facts extracted: nationally, Iran's nomads keep 58.5% of the country's sheep population and 39.7% of its goat population (Iran's total sheep=53.8M head [world rank 6th], goats=25M head [world rank 5th], FAO 2014 cited within the paper). Typical nomad family flock ~250 head, composition 89% goats / 8% sheep / 3% pack animals (horses/mules/donkeys/camels) (Ansari-Renani et al. 2013, cited within). Raeini cashmere goats: avg live weight 35kg (male)/30kg (female), average cashmere yield 507g/animal, 56.5% down yield, 19.5-micron fibre diameter. Kermani sheep (carpet-wool breed): avg 2.0kg wool/animal, 70% efficiency, 150mm staple length. This is genuine micro-level ('under the rocks') pastoral-economy data of exactly the kind the project brief asks for, complementing the national/provincial nomad population counts recorded separately in SOURCES.md Round 38.
